evidence.quote: The failed flight unit had been reworked, but the manufacturing procedural change had not been incorporated into the rework process.
An identical problem had been previously discovered that prompted a change to the manufacturing procedures to include a specified torque for the mounting screws.
Recurrence controls to prevent an original manufacturing defect should be expanded to address any items already deployed in field including those to be reprocessed or reworked.

impact_notes: Voltage fluctuation on AC2 buss during prelaunch checkout required inverter replacement; recurrence of previously identified manufacturing defect on deployed flight hardware created flight safety risk.

evidence.quote: a defective solder joint was found on a lead connection to a printed circuit board, analysis indicated the solder was contaminated with room temperature vulcanizer (RTV) silicone potting compound
this defect occurred prior to the requirement to train and certify the solder technicians to the aerospace quality standard DOD-STD-2000

impact_notes: Vent door actuator failure on STS-34 shuttle mission; actuator operated on only two of three electrical phases, requiring replacement. Defect traced to contaminated solder joint.

evidence.quote: The installation procedures did not include details for using sufficient lubrication on the cables being pulled through the conduit. Also, no cautions or warnings were provided to alert the electricians to take additional precautions
no information was available to the maintenance or operations personnel on the location of or access to shutoff systems
an emergency plan that did not include water leaks

impact_notes: Mission Control room flooded during maintenance; portion of facility rendered inoperable; mission-critical area damaged; emergency response hampered by inadequate planning and personnel knowledge of shutoff systems.

evidence.quote: Lack of fidelity between ground test system configuration and the flight system configuration can result in costly and damaging test failures to flight hardware during ground testing.
The skidding was caused by the brakes locking due to the failure of the anti-skid system. This failure was due to the use of non-flight switching valves as part of the test stand.
An investigation also discovered an error in the abort software and the addition of excessively restrictive non-flight orifices in the brake hydralic lines.

impact_notes: Flight hardware damage to two shuttle orbiter tires (blowouts), hydraulic hose, and quick disconnect fitting during ground test; costly test failure and potential schedule impact.

evidence.quote: Inadequate strain relief design for electrical cables can result in electrical shorts and subsequent ignition within closed environments such as the shuttle orbiter crew cabin.
The resultant flexure of the cable caused 360 degree annular breaks in the insulation of the power wires adjacent to each other.
The wire insulation material was changed from kapton to the more flexible teflon.

impact_notes: Fire/ignition hazard in crew cabin with potential crew exposure to electrical hazards and toxic contaminants from insulation burn.

evidence.quote: The panel installation procedures did not clearly identify the requirement for the washers.
Six flat washers were not installed under the mounting fasteners. This caused damage to the base panel material upon installation. The panel became loose during flight.
Incomplete assembly or installation instructions (no reference to the use of washers) can result in a poorly fastened exterior mounted component.

impact_notes: Panel separation during flight with potential for hazardous debris; no thermal protection system damage reported but risk to orbiter and crew; ~100 panels across fleet required inspection.

evidence.quote: growth of the structural skin of the wall due to pressurization during flight caused the failure
An object as large as a TV monitor detaching from its mount during any phase of a mission could become a hazard to personnel or equipment
Deformation or growth of base material (wall of orbiter crew cabin) from pressurization can cause debonding of hardware attached with adhesives

impact_notes: TV monitor detachment posed hazard to crew safety and equipment; required structural modification (wall doublers) to affected orbiter to prevent recurrence.

evidence.quote: Manufacturing practices allow some overdrill at the intersection of these two passages, which may have formed a stagnant volume at the base of the 'V'. This stagnant volume allows compression heating (adiabatic compression detonation) and/o
all five other regulators disassembled and inspected showed some particulate debris within the system
Failure to consider resistance to ignition and a tendency to propagate a fire in the selection of materials for use in high pressure oxygen may lead to fire, injury, and equipment loss

impact_notes: Flash fire during EMU performance test caused equipment damage and created serious safety risk to personnel; high-temperature oxygen-rich aluminum fire with potential for injury or loss of life.

evidence.quote: Use of assembly lubricant materials with suspended particles too large to pass through system filters can cause clogging
origin of the particles was traced to the krytox fluoropolymer thread lubricant grease used during loop assembly
system filters were clogged with teflon particles...reduce flow rates below minimum requirements

impact_notes: In-flight coolant system degradation requiring disassembly, cleaning, and replacement of filters, valves, flow proportioning module, and pump assembly; mission thermal control compromised.

evidence.quote: The unit was repaired without investigating the cause of the joint failure.
Repairing a failed component before investigating the cause of a failure can destroy evidence important to the investigation.
Rework procedures were changed to provide an inspection of failed parts before attempting repairs.

impact_notes: Loss of failure investigation evidence prevented root cause analysis and effective recurrence controls for solder joint fractures in critical flight computer hardware.

evidence.quote: Visual and holographic inspections of critical use tires can fail to reveal defects such as embedded wood chips and lead to tire failure. X-Rays may be required.
The wood chip came from the cart used to transport the tread gum rubber to the tire builder and was built into the tire.
The wood chip caused a delamination sufficient to produce a tire failure under some worst case, but feasible, landing scenarios (maximum permissible cross wind at KSC).

impact_notes: Embedded wood chip could cause tire failure during maximum crosswind landing conditions at KSC, though redundant NLG tire would provide backup support. Safety-critical defect in orbiter landing gear requiring fleet-wide inspection and process change.

evidence.quote: Inadequate leak check requirements may allow defects such as cross threaded fittings to remain undetected.
Procedural requirements were changed to include leak checks on fittings after initial installation and any time they are opened or disconnected.
During disassembly, a coolant inlet filter interface fitting was found to be cross threaded. It was concluded that this fitting leaked during ascent and while on orbit.

impact_notes: APU coolant system failure during ascent and on-orbit operations; coolant leak caused ice blockage that could have resulted in loss of auxiliary power unit functionality during critical mission phases.

evidence.quote: Moisture being trapped and held between the filter and its support screens had corroded the filter elements thereby causing the filter to fail and allowing contamination to migrate to the QD's
Sampling of the fluid had been conducted at the GSE cart upstream of the filter...there are many types of contamination and corrosion present in the QD's
Leaking of nitrogen from the APU fuel tank could potentially result in the inability to restart the APU for orbiter re-entry

impact_notes: Potential loss of APU restart capability during orbiter re-entry, creating critical safety risk to crew and vehicle.

evidence.quote: heater, which was not equipped with a safety device to shut it off automatically in case of high temperature caused by the water level falling below the level of the heater element
all equipment with the potential for overheating or causing a fire should be intrinsically safe, and there should be adequate safeguards to disconnect power in an anomalous situation
heater was inadvertently left on overnight

impact_notes: Fire damage estimated at $350,000 to NASA facility; potential for personnel injury and facility loss.

evidence.quote: Different size fittings require different torque values, which the specifications did not identify.
The installation drawing does not specify that the other end of the flex hose has a smaller fitting that should require a lower torque.
one end of the flex hose was torqued to 120 foot pounds

impact_notes: Hydraulic fluid leakage in critical orbiter system; hose replacement and re-torquing required; potential flight safety risk if not caught during pre-mission inspection.

evidence.quote: the spring holding it closed was broken and had been installed backwards. The error in installation contributed to additional stresses on the spring during earlier flow tests
A mandatory inspection point was added to the fabrication assembly procedures to preclude installation errors for this spring
An incorrect installation of a critical component can cause additional stresses, lead to a material failure

impact_notes: Potential for uncontrolled flows during cryogenic operations could have caused launch delays; actual failure detected during ground testing before flight.

evidence.quote: A reassembly error (a dislocated wire pinched by the uninsulated area of a harness clamp) causing a short in a fuel line heater and a poorly located cut off switch can combine to cause a control failure
under some failure modes, a shorted condition could remain undetected by both the automatic cut-off sensors and the temperature indicators that alert the crew or ground controllers
When reassembled, the heater wire separated from the bundle, dislocated into the uninsulated part of the clamp, and was pinched by the clamp when it was tightened

impact_notes: Potential for hydrazine fuel auto-decomposition explosion causing severe damage to orbiter; catastrophic failure prevented only by crew manual intervention within 5-minute window.

evidence.quote: The cracks, in many cases, were not perceptible with the human eye and were only detected by Eddy Current inspection.
Cracks in components of critical flight hardware may go undetected during pre-flight visual inspection and can lead to hardware failure and equipment damage.
The retainers are made from Titanium/Zirconium/Molybdenum (TZM), which is very resistant to high temperature and very brittle.

impact_notes: Right hand inboard brake failure during STS-7 landing; brittle material (TZM) retainers cracked, causing hardware failure despite minimal braking action required.

evidence.quote: A modification was performed earlier to add a lug set and wiring to the panel that was not routed through the same remote controls location as the original wires, and was not reflected on the facility master drawing.
The injured electrician did not verify the lug was deenergized by testing with a meter or other device.

Undocumented drawing changes to hazardous systems (480 volts AC), coupled with failure to follow required verification of system deenergization as part of lockout/tagout procedure, can lead to potentially serious injuries.

impact_notes: Minor electrical burns sustained; potential for severe injury or death if current had passed through torso.

evidence.quote: The usual, but undocumented, step in the replacement procedure that called for component removal before changing the neck dam did not occur.

A small piece of fabric reinforced elastomer material was found lodged inside the controller...The piece of elastomer from the old neck dam remained in the suit as debris and migrated to the diaphragm during the pressure cycling.
No previous failures of this type had occurred before.

impact_notes: Loss of crew member if cabin pressurization failed during flight; potential catastrophic consequence during launch or entry phase.

evidence.quote: particle contamination within limit switches...particles were metallic substances...from metallic (weld expulsion), glass from headers, ceramic from insulators, rubber from back-filling equipment
primary corrective action to prevent recurrence of these failures was to impose Particle Impact Noise Detection (PIND) testing on all future limit switch manufacturing
Limit switches are often used to: (1) prevent systems or components from reaching damaging stress levels and, (2) inhibiting unwanted events or conditions

impact_notes: Multiple shuttle orbiter mission anomalies caused by contaminated limit switches; failures prevented critical safety functions (stress prevention, event inhibition) and resulted in loss of switch indication states, creating potential for system damage or unintended events.

evidence.quote: The sharp edges of zippers bearing against adjacent materials can wear and penetrate the materials, resulting in potentially critical failures
Three of the flotation assemblies were flown on the orbiter for two flights and used twice in training sequences, which resulted in the zippers wearing and cutting the bladder

impact_notes: Potential loss of crew emergency flotation capability during water landing; bladder failure could compromise rescue equipment function.

evidence.quote: If the door speed control valve is in the open or closed position and the flow valve is inadvertently moved out of neutral, the cargo bay doors will open or close.
Unscheduled operation of the payload canister doors could result in personnel injury or damage to a payload or canister door.
Install locking device on the flow control valve to hold neutral position until door speed valve is fully closed/locked upon desired door position.

impact_notes: Personnel injury or damage to payload/canister door possible from unscheduled door operation due to inadvertent valve manipulation.

evidence.quote: Disconnecting the flexhose under pressure may damage equipment and/or injure personnel.
Update OMI to remove GN2 pressure by opening control valves and verifying pressure on k-bottle or facility supply, prior to disconnecting flexhose.
high GN2 pressure may be present in the flexhose between the k-bottle or facility GN2 supply and the remote control console

impact_notes: Personnel injury and equipment damage risk from pressurized gas release during routine maintenance operations.

evidence.quote: Unmonitored payload processing and propellant transfer operations may result in personnel exposure to toxic hypergolic fluids/vapors.
Monitor concentrations of hypergolic vapor prior to, during, and after all hypergol operations/perform GSE leak checks to minimize potential spills.
Leakage or spilling of toxic propellants is a real possibility during payload ground operations and may allow propellant residuals to accumulate in/on equipment/flight hardware.

impact_notes: Personnel exposure to toxic hypergolic fluids and vapors during payload processing operations poses direct safety risk to ground operations staff.

evidence.quote: Failure of a critical item will disable the sweep flow purge to the vent stack, which is performed prior to initiating hydrogen steady state venting from the dewar.
Failure/corrosion/clogging of critical hardware in fuel cell servicing service system panels may cause ineffective purge and/or explosion.
Redesign purge system to modify purge path and relocate interface with vent system to provide adequate purge capability.

impact_notes: Failure of purge system critical hardware could result in ineffective purge operations prior to hydrogen venting, creating explosion hazard during launch operations.

evidence.quote: There is a possibility of nitrogen intrusion into the oxygen feed line if a regulator failure occurs.
Failure of ECLSS servicing console regulator could introduce gaseous nitrogen into astronaut's breathing supply.
Redesign the ECLSS GO2 panels to eliminate the use of gaseous nitrogen while supporting the flight crew breathing test.

impact_notes: Potential for nitrogen intrusion into astronaut breathing supply during pre-flight test poses direct safety risk to flight crew.

evidence.quote: Failure of relay K41 to activate or relay K44 to deactivate will disable hardwire safing panel control of both the primary and secondary extend pilot valves
Design a redundant hardwire control of the primary and secondary pilot valves using individual relays to control each valve function
The command path for the hardwire safing circuits, which controls the orbiter access arm, runs through these relays

impact_notes: Single relay failure disables both primary and secondary extend pilot valves, preventing OAA extension and emergency re-extension capability—critical safety and mission impact.

evidence.quote: ropes are used to lower the platforms to the orbiter and technicians have minimal control of the speed at which the platforms are lowered
A potential exists for a loss of platform control and subsequent orbiter damage/personnel injury
Provide winch and cable arrangement for safely extending/retracting platforms

impact_notes: Potential for major orbiter damage and personnel injury from uncontrolled platform fall during mate/demate operations.

evidence.quote: The adapter is designed for the tank to sit flush in the cradle, but its configuration allows only the GN2 tank boss threads to sit on the adapter cradle. As a result, the tank boss supports the total tank load.
The lifting adapter prohibits tank from sitting flush in cradle due to a 1.8" tank boss extension, allowing boss threads to support total load.
The strap securing the GN2 tank to the lifting adapter provides an inadequate securing capability during lifting operations.

impact_notes: Potential for damage to GN2 tank, orbiter payload bay, and personnel injury during lifting operations.

evidence.quote: Because there are no work platforms or approved tie-off points where a safety lanyard/harness can be attached, technicians are at risk of a 255-foot fall.
technicians to perform this task at unprotected heights

impact_notes: Personnel safety hazard with potential for fatal 255-foot fall during routine maintenance operations.

evidence.quote: The start/stop controls and circuit breakers...are located in the motor control center at the RSS 215-foot level; therefore, there is no local control.
The start/stop controls and circuit breakers can not be locked out, physically, to prevent inadvertant actuation.
When maintenance personnel are working on the pumps, a possibility exists that someone could re-apply power.

impact_notes: Potential for maintenance personnel injury or fatality due to inadvertent pump actuation from displaced controls lacking physical lockout capability.

evidence.quote: The system design does not include a secondary means to secure the weight if the winch or wire rope fails
workers shore the weights with pieces of wood or other available material that will brace the weights. The improperly used shoring devices could break under the load
A withdrawal weight support failure during shock absorber maintenance may result in personnel injury/fatality

impact_notes: Potential personnel injury or fatality from 1500-pound weight collapse during maintenance operations.

evidence.quote: A leak/fire during cryogenic loading or draining operations may result in loss of vehicle and/or life. This risk remains active until shuttle liftoff
LH2 leaks may occur via structure/component failure, purge loss due to flow restriction (clogs, regulator/valve fails closed), or flow diversion (relief valve fails open, flexhose ruptures)
Minimize leakage by utilizing welded connections, pneumatic systems for controlling cryogenic valves, and purging system for valves flowing LH2

impact_notes: Loss of shuttle vehicle and/or loss of personnel life during cryogenic loading/draining operations on the pad.

evidence.quote: NSI's are unprotected against excessive radio frequency (RF) fields during pyrotechnic operations/post-landing safing and may result in injury/damage.
NSI's can not be protected against inadvertant detonation while in excessive radio frequency fields. The electromagnetic interference may cause a premature firing of a NSI
Closely monitor pyrotechnic connection, testing, and safing operations, to ensure rf field levels are maintained within safety plan guidelines.

impact_notes: Potential for personnel injury/loss of life and damage to flight hardware or ground support equipment from premature NSI detonation due to uncontrolled RF exposure.

evidence.quote: In the event of regulator diaphragm failure, GH2 or GO2 can vent to atmosphere near the orbiter through the back pressure regulators. Leaking GH2 could cause a fire or explosion.
Install an isolation valve upstream of the back pressure regulator, thereby allowing manual vent line pressure regulation.

impact_notes: Potential fire or explosion hazard from GH2 venting near orbiter post-landing; single-point failure in PRSD T-0 safing system with no isolation capability.

evidence.quote: Loss of GN2 purge to the vacuum servicing unit could result in fire or explosion leading to flight hardware damage or injury to personnel.
Design a GN2 pressure sensing safety interlock switch that will inhibit use of the vacuum servicing unit when GN2 purge is not present.

impact_notes: Potential fire/explosion hazard with risk of flight hardware damage and personnel injury during ground servicing operations.

evidence.quote: Failure to establish controlled venting of PRSD tanks prior to exceeding tank relief pressures, could cause inadvertant venting and danger of fire.
Ensure all OMI's are updated to establish controlled venting at the SLF (or other safe area) after landing and prior to OPF roll-in.
This venting has occurred during roll-in of the orbiter to the OPF and could cause a fire if an ignition source is present.

impact_notes: Potential fire hazard during orbiter processing in OPF due to uncontrolled GH2 venting; safety risk to personnel and vehicle.

evidence.quote: If mass SPEC vacuum is lost for any reason (power loss or belt breakage) vacuum roughing pump oil may be sucked into the SSME heat exchanger (back-streaming) unless the hex is isolated immediately.
Design and install vacuum controlled isolation valves that will prevent contamination of SSME oxygen lines, in the event of loss of pump vacuum.

impact_notes: Contamination of SSME oxygen lines with pump oil could cause engine failure or safety hazard during shuttle pre-start or operation; affects flight readiness and mission safety.

evidence.quote: Space shuttle system design necessitated clearance and weight constraints, which in many cases, made SSME access limited.
Injury to personnel or damage to equipment can occur as the result of actions required to perform post flight maintenance and ferry flight set installation operations.
Post landing access to SSME's for installation of the ferry flight kit is inadequate and could cause personnel injury.

impact_notes: Risk of personnel injury and equipment damage during post-flight maintenance and ferry flight operations due to inadequate engine access design.

evidence.quote: OMI emergency procedures for powering down fuel cells assume redundant ground power is connected to the orbiter and calls for only one disconnect sequence fuel cell 1, 2, then 3.

ensure that an operating bus is the last one disconnected. Failure to maintain GSE helium pressure below vehicle reactant pressure when fuel cells are operating may result in FC degradation or failure.
Instruct orbiter integrity clerks (OIC), electrical techs/space craft operators and operations personnel to monitor main bus voltage and alert test team upon occurrence of low voltage.

impact_notes: Loss of vehicle data downlink to firing room; fire alarm and electrical component malfunctions; fuel cell degradation/failure; loss of power during emergency procedures with no ground power backup.

evidence.quote: Use of a conductive lanyard to arm payloads having internal power sources may introduce a potential current path for a sneak circuit.
The current design of FFAR's does not provide a reliable electrical ground connection between the fins and the nozzle plate.
Aircraft rocket launcher assemblies may produce a potentially dangerous galvanic current if dissimilar metals are used in fabrication.

impact_notes: Unintended rocket motor ignition during payload arming procedure with personnel in immediate vicinity; high probability of fatality or serious injury.

evidence.quote: technicians did not follow the sequence of events specified in the PR disposition for assembly of the thruster and fixture
vinyl tape acted as a lubricant between the clamped surfaces. These conditions allowed the offset weight...to rotate about the single C-clamp and fall
Ensure that any surfaces that are clamped together are clean and free of tape, tape adhesive residue, oil, dirt, or other lubricating elements

impact_notes: Thruster and fixture assembly fell, resulting in potential damage to hardware and safety risk to personnel in work area.

evidence.quote: Pipes with less than adequate support will rupture or bend excessively when external forces are applied.
Utility annex water lines routed above unprotected high voltage electrical equipment may rupture causing power outages and equipment failures.
Operations and maintenance instructions for critical utility systems must identify potential emergency conditions and provide emergency procedures.

impact_notes: Power outage suspended test operations on the Hubble Space Telescope; multiple facility systems affected by loss of electrical power due to water damage to motor control center.

evidence.quote: Remote location of control rooms creates a natural barrier to communications and observation of work.
Inconsistent or misleading use of nomenclature that does not conform to the OMI may cause confusion for technicians as they perform OMI procedures.
Unfinished PR's that require disconnecting wires to other systems create operations constraints which may be transparent to systems engineers.

impact_notes: Communications barriers and nomenclature inconsistencies led to inadvertent capping of purge/vent ports and untracked system constraints affecting orbiter processing efficiency and test operations.

evidence.quote: Improper assembly and adjustment of boom hoist brake releasing mechanism may allow the boom and load to lower rapidly and uncontrollably.
A crane with a history of problems, including 'boom running', caused a mishap because the past problems were not properly reported.
Neglecting to add the weight of heavy rigging and the weight of the crane load block will result in a miscalculated load.

impact_notes: Personnel injury (sprained foot) and equipment damage to payload canister; near-miss potential for serious injury or fatality from falling boom and load.

evidence.quote: Mercoid pressure switches are overly sensitive to system pressure fluctuations and may cause inadvertent activation (switch bouncing).
Water deluge systems are unable to adequately absorb water line transients, thus jeopardizing the integrity of the valves & pump controller switches.
The switch activation was caused by potable water system pressure fluctuations external to the local facility.

impact_notes: Inadvertent fire pump activation and deluge valve opening caused by pressure transients; potential for unintended water discharge affecting facility operations and equipment integrity.

evidence.quote: System components are not all identified with find number tags, allowing for erroneous component selection during maintenance operations.
The lack of coordinated numbers between components and the maintenance instructions generated a confusing atmosphere and led the technicians to choose the wrong components.
This action resulted in the erroneous activation of the VPF Firex water deluge system.

impact_notes: Unintended activation of fire suppression system; potential equipment damage and operational disruption at VPF facility.

evidence.quote: The remote manual arming and firing valves are in the "on" position when perpendicular to the water lines, which is not the industry norm for hydraulic systems.
OPF GSE technicians believing they were closing the valves by positioning them perpendicular to the water line when, in fact, they were opening the valves.
The OPF Firex deluge remote manual arming and firing valves do not conform to industry norms, allowing for misinterpretation of their configuration.

impact_notes: Unintended activation of Firex deluge system zones 1-5 in bay 2 of OPF; potential equipment damage and operational disruption from water discharge.

evidence.quote: Wiring harness connectors of critical components must be capable of withstanding vibration induced stresses. Failure to protect wiring harnesses from abrasive surfaces and sharp edges of structural members will lead to wire damage caused by
Identify and implement improvements in the quality review, inspection, and testing of connectors in proportion to their criticality.
Prior to future launches, perform megger checks of all critical wiring harnesses and inspect harnesses to verify quality of installation.

impact_notes: Premature shutdown of main and vernier engines during flight due to power bus short circuit caused by vibration-induced wiring insulation damage.

evidence.quote: The installation design of the dry well and temperature transducer provides a means to remove the transducer from the system...The dry well installation design is inadequate to preclude an untrained technician or a careless technician from
Adequate physical safeguards do not exist to prevent the inadvertent removal of dry wells/transducer installations from hazardous systems
Failure to test for hazardous levels of MMH within the sump tank, resulted in the release of contaminated waste water to the environment

impact_notes: Major hypergol fuel spill with personnel injury (hypergol burns to technician); environmental contamination from untested hazardous waste release to sump tank.

evidence.quote: No formal briefings on associated hazards were held, allowing for unknown hazards to jeopardize the safety of the project and the workers.
The "Call Contractor" is often required to work with incomplete drawings & written instructions, possibly creating hazardous conditions.
Lack of formal training for complicated platform operators and observers may allow some hazards to go unnoticed until a mishap occurs.

impact_notes: Safety hazard to workers and project integrity due to incomplete hazard communication, inadequate documentation, and lack of formal operator training and certification.

evidence.quote: Technicians attempting to mate connectors in areas of restricted visibility can mismate connectors.
Revise connector procedures to permit greater visibility or redesign connectors to preclude inadvertent connections.
Kapton wire insulation can breakdown and sustain an electrical arc, igniting the insulation and causing damage.

impact_notes: Battery damage, connector damage, thermal blanket damage, and electrical arcs from short circuit created by mismate; spacecraft system failure risk.

evidence.quote: Failure of an OMI to require calculation of quantities of N2O4 to be released could result in loss of life or personnel injury.
Post incident calculations estimated the total release to have been 28 gallons. The vapor cloud of N2O4 far exceeded the 700-foot clear area called out in the OMI.
Maintenance for SCAPE Suits does not include checking for pinhole leaks, allowing SCAPE suit deterioration to go unnoticed, endangering personnel.

impact_notes: Potential loss of life or personnel injury from N2O4 vapor exposure due to inadequate hazard area prediction and SCAPE suit integrity failures during hazardous maintenance operations.

evidence.quote: A design flaw in the quick disconnects provides an additional leak path, allowing liquid to flow through the QD and exit in the release collar area.
The failure of the ground-half QD was attributed to an accumulation of iron nitrate lodged between the probe and the dynamic head. The presence of iron nitrate was traced to contaminated fuel.

Modify procedures to include testing for fuel contamination prior to flow through the system. SCAPE technicians need ready access to fire fighting equipment when working with hazardous/combustible materials.

impact_notes: 15-20 gallons of N2O4 oxidizer spilled during servicing; hazardous material release with potential safety and environmental consequences.

evidence.quote: Current design allows the possibility of Q.D. nipple removal while trying to remove Q.D. cap.
Lack of anti-rotational devices may allow Q.D. nipple to separate from the Q.D. when a technician attempts to remove the Q.D. cap.
The technician had no visual clue that the nipple was turning because of the scupper installation.

impact_notes: 3.5 gallon hydrazine spill onto MLP deck; technician exposure to toxic propellant requiring decontamination; operational delay for servicing and cleanup.

evidence.quote: The locations of the HAD's (roof beam mid-line, one at apex and one each half-way down on roof underside) are unlikely to detect fires occurring near the farm edges, where much of the hypergolic maintenance is performed.

Fire detection system high temperature detectors must be located near areas where conflagration is likely. The fire detection system high temperature detectors did not activate, resulting in no automatic fire suppression system activation.

Lack of fire alarm activation boxes along the evacuation route could prevent egressing personnel from activating the system. SCAPE Van personnel were not properly equipped or prepared to take contaminated personnel onboard during a spill/fi

impact_notes: 20-gallon MMH spill with spontaneous combustion and fire; detection system failure prevented automatic suppression activation, creating life-safety hazard for personnel and facility.

evidence.quote: The clamp was not properly tethered. Continuing emphasis is required to ensure that personnel are knowledgeable of and comply with tethering requirements.
Personal equipment used by repair personnel in elevated positions must be tethered to them to prevent damage from dropping.
Incorporate specific instructions in work authorization documents to tether or otherwise restrain all equipment used in elevated positions.

impact_notes: Equipment fell 16 floors near personnel in transfer isle, creating significant safety hazard with potential for serious injury or fatality.

evidence.quote: Failure to power up the orbiter during ferry flight stopovers disables the thruster heaters and increases the potential for RCS thruster leakage.
Due to an excessively slow panel lamp, the exhaust system was actuated several times and resulted in the system remaining off.
RCS thruster valve seals have a history of sensitivity to temperatures below 60 deg F.

impact_notes: Technician exposure to monomethylhydrazine (MMH) fuel spill (1/4 to 3/4 cup) contacting skin and clothing; safety hazard from toxic propellant handling during ferry operations.

evidence.quote: Upper limit switches being used as operational stops can not be relied upon to stop the operation during an emergency.
Use of an inadequate tagout or lockout device may allow improper restoration of a system previously tagged as hazardous.
Hoist systems (as used in the OPF) can release excessive lengths of cable from the spool.

impact_notes: Platform fell during orbiter closeout operations, damaging left payload bay door and injuring technician. Cable failure resulted from inadequate lockout compliance and design deficiencies in hoist system.

evidence.quote: the crane operator was attempting to raise the hook until a load of 11,000 pounds was attained, but the load cell read zero
the requirement for its usage is not clear. Loadcell usage is not mentioned in the crane OMRSD nor is it mentioned in the system assurance analysis
Institute/write procedures for the operation, maintenance, and calibration of load cell usage

impact_notes: Forward center segment of solid rocket motor fractured during handling operations, resulting in hardware damage and operational delay.

evidence.quote: fillet welds on tube "D" provided an outward ramp effect that caused excessive wear on the keepers and the eventual bowing of tube "C"
normal operations required bypassing the "up" limit switch and using observers to determine when to stop the upward travel
Excessive wear of the load bearing surfaces on the keepers of tube "C" was the result of impact during approximately 30 retraction cycles

impact_notes: Three sections of telescoping tube assembly fell, severely damaging the rotating service structure and orbital maneuvering system pod covers.

evidence.quote: Failure to utilize redundant overtemp cutoff circuitry may result in damage to components undergoing baking/curing processes.
The modification incorporates a direct 440 VAC cut-off initiated by series redundant over-temperature shutoff circuits, as well as, a series redundant shutoff of the temperature control.
It was discovered that the oven had failed, allowing the temperature to reach an estimated 1400 deg F.

impact_notes: Three injector/chamber assemblies damaged by severe oxidation due to oven temperature excursion from 600°F to ~1400°F during cerachrome insulation baking process.

evidence.quote: Emergency stop switch covers made of soft or pliable material allowing inadvertent actuation of emergency stop switch.
Lack of integrated emergency stop control switches allowed the tow tug to proceed when the trailing support vehicle initiated an emergency stop.
the observer caused the rig to suddenly stop when his hand inadvertently came in contact with the E-STOP switch cover

impact_notes: Loss of freon 14 coolant from orbiter support system; environmental release; potential damage to orbiter thermal management systems and operational delay.

evidence.quote: Electrical terminals of mercury switches located too close to the chassis, result in electrical arcing and mercury switch explosion.
a mercury switch mounted close to the chassis inside the front panel arced and exploded

impact_notes: Mercury switch explosion during routine maintenance inspection; potential for personnel injury and equipment damage.

evidence.quote: 4 of the 8 valves used in the system were not designed for use in vacuum systems
air was ingested into the vent line through a valve stem. The ingested air froze, forming a solid blockage
Ensure all dewars are designed and fitted with valves designed to be used in systems utilizing vacuum pressures

impact_notes: Dewar rupture damaged facility infrastructure (ceiling tiles, copy machine) and posed safety risk to personnel and payload experiments.

evidence.quote: Soft metal (aluminum) rivets used to secure steel hinges to heavy platforms failed, resulting in flight hardware damage.
The present practice of securing the hinge mounts with pop rivets is not adequate to support these platforms.
All hinge platform mounts need to be secured by welds or bolts and lock washers.

impact_notes: Flight hardware damage to orbiter OV-102 left wing; platform fell during retraction operations, creating safety hazard and requiring repair.

evidence.quote: Using an unprotected external high-intensity lamp for illumination above sensitive flight hardware resulted in damage when the lamp exploded.
Alignment of the HUT was not a scheduled activity at KSC. Therefore a light source was not furnished with the experiment.
Lead engineers should approve only protected light sources when there is a potential for damage to flight hardware.

impact_notes: Flight hardware (HUT experiment) contaminated by shattered lamp bulb fragments, requiring re-alignment and rework.

evidence.quote: An explosion proof connector inadvertently faulted, causing an arc flashburn to the technicians' eyes.
Develop a test plan to study these connectors to determine the useful life expectancy (MTBF); then replace them before a more severe mishap occurs.
One employee attempted to manually twist a receptacle with a screwdriver. During this action the receptacle faulted either phase to phase or phase to ground.

impact_notes: Arc flash burn injuries to both technicians' eyes; potential for more severe mishap if connector failure not addressed through MTBF testing and replacement program.

evidence.quote: The explosion was caused when the connectors' overheated multi-point contact band caused the solder to melt, destroying the connectors' Integrity.
The power cable connectors used to provide power to the portable purge unit (PPU) require an excessive amount of force to mate them to the receptacles.
During an explosion and fire of a 480 volt power cable connector, employees were not provided with a means to remove power from the cables.

impact_notes: Connector explosion and fire with potential to seriously injure or kill personnel within 50-foot radius; caused portable purge unit shutdown during operations.

evidence.quote: Inadvertent malfunction of crown manaboards steering control electronics, caused the crown manaboard to jump off the control track.
Redesign the crown manaboard steering control electronics unit to reduce the possibility of future mishaps.

The operator had already made several issues and was in the process of returning materials to their location. He was looking at the location of his next issue and when he looked up the manaboard was heading for the racks.

impact_notes: Facility damage to shelving support structure; operational disruption to logistics materials handling; potential safety risk to personnel from equipment malfunction.

evidence.quote: Lack of an open circuit shut off switch resulted with an electric arc cracking a debrazing head and damaging a coil and purge fittings.
During debrazing operations of a purge fitting in an orbiter's aft compartment, the debrazing head separated, causing an electrical arc.

impact_notes: Equipment damage to debrazing head, coil, and purge fittings during orbiter maintenance operations; safety hazard from uncontrolled electrical arc.

evidence.quote: the second layer of cable caused the end ring welds to fail, breaking the cable, and dropping the load
During a platform hoisting proof load test, the second layer of cable caused the end ring welds to fail
Verify that all critical welds have been performed in accordance with specification 79K30047, SEC. 0510

impact_notes: Platform flipped and fell to ground after load drop during proof test; potential for personnel injury and equipment damage during operational use if defect not corrected.

evidence.quote: Failure of clutch to provide a positive engagement between the drum, shaft, gear train, and break allowed the hoist's drum to begin free wheeling.
Modify the hoist's clutch assembly to ensure that it provides a positive mechanical engagement that will prevent free wheeling.

impact_notes: Technician fell 15 feet from 155-foot level, sustaining broken left ankle and minor bruises. Safety-critical failure of load-holding mechanism on personnel lift.

evidence.quote: The absence of tie-off points or protective holding areas for hand portable equipment resulted in damage when breakout boxes were knocked off a work platform.
Three breakout boxes were located on the orbiter aft compartment entrance platform...falling 7.5 feet and striking the space shuttle main engine (SSME) #1 AIR/GN2 purge line.

impact_notes: Damage to fiberglass purge line and SSME #1 main fuel position actuator; potential mission delay and repair costs.

evidence.quote: Incomplete documentation and instructions resulted in one of the orbiter's insulation blankets being damaged by air flow from an ECS duct.
air flowing from a nearby duct was blowing against a thermal protection system (TPS) blanket causing irreparable damage
Provide appropriate inputs to applicable standard practice instructions and work authorizing documents.

impact_notes: Thermal protection system blanket sustained irreparable damage requiring replacement; potential safety risk if TPS integrity compromised on orbiter.

evidence.quote: OMS/RCS Systems program control logic does not reset each time the program is activated, allowing inadvertent over-pressurization of the system.
The computer program indicated the incorrect status of the system because the control logic limits were not adjusted/reset prior to starting the operation.
Add a step to all OMS/RCS system OMI's that are monitored by control logic, to adjust logic limits prior to changing pressure.

impact_notes: Burst disk rupture due to pressure spike to ~400 psig; system over-pressurization hazard with potential for propellant tank failure and mission-critical OMS/RCS system loss.

evidence.quote: Lack of redundant high temperature cut-off switches allowed 3 RCS thrusters to be over heated during insulation baking process.

quality assurance was not notified. As a result, the full extent of damage was not determined until the severe discoloration of titanium alloy and columbium parts was noted during the post-baking weighing process, about three weeks later.
oven temperature control system malfunctioned and permitted the temperature to reach an estimated 1400 deg F during a cerachrome insulation baking process that should have been limited to 625 deg F.

impact_notes: Three RCS primary thruster injector/chamber assemblies damaged; titanium alloy and columbium parts severely discolored; three-week delay in damage discovery.

evidence.quote: hazardous GN2 purge orifice at valve A89406 was blocked by corrosion and foreign contamination
Provide a pressure relief valve in this area of the system
section of line was inadvertently over-pressurized, resulting in a protective cap being blown approximately 350 feet away

impact_notes: Projectile hazard (cap blown 350 feet); system over-pressurization during critical LH2 flow test preparation; safety risk to personnel and facilities.

evidence.quote: Failure to fully engage the puller bar prior to detensioning a holddown post, resulted in failure of the puller bar socket-to-holddown post threads.
With only 8 threads engaged, the shear stresses increased to 96,000 psi, as compared to 98,000 psi shear strength of the stud.
Include a requirement in the OMI to take a measurement verifying that the puller bar is fully engaged prior to start of detensioning operation.

impact_notes: Hardware damage to SRB holddown stud requiring replacement; operational delay during detensioning; potential safety risk if stud failure had occurred under different load conditions.

evidence.quote: The victim was familiar and educated to the proper safety precautions...for some unknown reason the victim failed to connect the ground wire
Train technicians to check for and establish a path to ground prior to and after maintenance operations

impact_notes: Personnel electrocution injury during elevator installation at Cape Canaveral facility.

evidence.quote: engineer positioned ullage valve (V4) to closed, this caused a pressure spike that ruptured the valve burst disk. The engineer was unaware of the failure
Put indications in the operating instructions of exact operation end point, and require safety buy-off to ensure compliance
manifolds were vented to ambient pressure. This allowed thrusters to leak

impact_notes: Valve burst disk failure with oxidizer vapor release; failure discovered post-event during testing and data review rather than during operations.

evidence.quote: noise level of the PPU when operating exceeds the recommended 85 DBA...DBA level near the unit ranges from 91.0 DBA to 99.6 DBA
Ensure the operations instruction document requires the use of hearing protection prior to start up of the PPU

impact_notes: Occupational health hazard: noise exposure 6–15 dB above safe limits within 30-foot operational perimeter poses hearing damage risk to personnel without proper controls.

evidence.quote: No automatic monitoring and/or control exists to preclude simultaneous application of ECS purge air while all orbiter vent doors are closed
Require appropriate operations instructions to verify that all orbiter vent doors are in the standard purge configuration prior to start of venting
Inadvertent closure of the vent doors during environmental control system (ECS) purge of orbiter compartments will result in over-pressurization

impact_notes: Over-pressurization of orbiter compartments could damage equipment, structures, or systems; potential safety risk to vehicle and personnel during ground operations.

evidence.quote: The existing lifting sling design induces stress into the unit's operating components and overstresses the unit's lift points.
Cracks are present on the lifting lug plates and elongation of the lifting lug holes has occurred.
Redesign lifting sling mechanism to prevent stress from being applied to the PPU's operating components during lifting operations.

impact_notes: Structural damage to portable purge units and potential damage to orbiter systems and ground support equipment; repeated lifting operations caused progressive cracking and component degradation.

evidence.quote: When maintenance personnel are working on the fans, it is possible that someone could re-apply power to them.
Inadvertent actuation of ECS fans S1 and S2 at the payload changeout room (PCR) during maintenance will cause injury or death of personnel.
Add a requirement to the operations instruction manual requiring the technicians to lock out electrical power prior to beginning maintenance.

impact_notes: Potential injury or death of maintenance personnel due to inadvertent fan actuation during maintenance operations.

evidence.quote: Present talkback verification method for the payload retention latch assembly (PRLA) involves extensive "hot" pin jumpering during testing using a 79K07833 breakout box and test leads.
Improper jumper procedure by inserting a jumper at an incorrect location could result in inadvertent shorts or cross connection of live 120 volt and 28 volt electrical lines.
Design a status tester and jumper verification device to ensure proper jumper connections of the PRLA prior to interfacing with the orbiter.

impact_notes: Risk of electrical shock/personnel injury, PRLA electrical system damage, and orbiter interface damage from improper jumper connections during live voltage testing.

evidence.quote: Extension of the -Y curtain wall with the -Y strut in any position except fully extended, the curtain wall could contact the STS vehicle or the RSS structure. This can result from human error or human error coupled with mechanical failure o
Design redundant interlock system to ensure the -Y curtain wall is fully retracted and locked in its support frame prior to moving the -Y strut.
This can result from human error or human error coupled with mechanical failure of the key-lock and interlock valves.

impact_notes: Potential contact and damage to STS vehicle and RSS structure; safety risk to orbiter and ground support equipment.

evidence.quote: Ensure all lines are maintained with proper insulation and all surfaces are visually inspected for excessive ice build-up prior to clear for launch.
During launch ice may be dislodged by vibroacoustic or aerodynamic forces and impact the orbiter's windows or TPS with enough force to cause damage.

impact_notes: Ice impact on orbiter windows or thermal protection system during ascent poses critical safety risk to vehicle structural integrity and crew safety.

evidence.quote: Loss of radio communications during SCAPE operations has resulted in injury to personnel.
When and if communications are lost, the operation shall be terminated immediately and will not resume until communications are restored.
Ensure all personnel involved in SCAPE operations have backup radio communication systems available prior to starting the SCAPE operation.

impact_notes: Personnel injury occurred due to loss of radio communications during SCAPE operations.

evidence.quote: Inadvertent connection/disconnection of ET electrical components with power applied, could cause sparks, damaging flight hardware or injuring personnel.

Connections are connected within the intertank by electrical plugs and receptacles, which are secured by threaded sleeves. Power and return lines are not routed through adjacent pins on the same connector.
Verify and ensure all connectors meet MIL STD with shielded pins on male connectors to preclude personnel contact.

impact_notes: Potential ignition of SRB and damage to flight hardware or personnel injury from inadvertent electrical arcing during ground operations.

evidence.quote: Determining the center of gravity of spent booster segments prior to a lift operation is very difficult, often allowing dangerous conditions to exist.
The instability of the segment is due to the difficulty in determining its center of gravity.
Design a handling sling device that will self-adjust to the load as it is lifted and keep it steady throughout the lifting operation.

impact_notes: Segment suspension failure during transport with 90% of load suspended 10-15 feet above rail car created serious personnel safety hazard; near-miss with potential for catastrophic drop and injury.

evidence.quote: Pop rivets used to secure access door hinges frequently work their way out leaving the door and hinges dangerously loose.
The door started working loose approximately three weeks prior to the incident, and a trouble call was made. Blue tape was found on the door edges except for the hinged side.
Hinged access panels frequently lose structural integrity near the hinge line and may break off.

impact_notes: Access panel fell during OMS pod removal operations; no direct vehicle damage but created FOD hazard and operational risk. Potential for injury or vehicle damage if panel had struck personnel or critical hardware.

evidence.quote: Update operational procedures to include a requirement to ensure proper hose configuration and routing prior to beginning purge operations.
The routing and hose length was left over from a previous operation on the left side of the orbiter.
Failure to require a specific hose route resulted in a liquid trap being inadvertently formed causing liquid MMH to leak from a thruster nozzle.

impact_notes: Hypergolic fuel (MMH) leak required high bay evacuation, emergency fan actuation, and fire/medical standby response. Safety hazard to personnel and facility.

evidence.quote: Failure to measure and verify adequate clearance, prior to platform extension could result in damage to orbiter thermal protection tiles.
Review platform extension procedures to ensure verification of ample clearance prior to extending platforms.
the observer noted that the platform would not clear the body flap and signaled a halt. Before the platform could be stopped, it contacted two tiles, damaging them.

impact_notes: Damage to orbiter thermal protection tiles requiring repair; potential schedule impact for tile replacement and re-verification.

evidence.quote: Develop procedures that require the use of hydrasets and dynamometers when lifting unknown amounts of weight.
a small block with two 5/8" bolts apparently sheared, allowing Z Bar to travel up, contacting a portion of the upper floating beam
Review platform extension procedures to ensure verification of ample clearance prior to extending platforms.

impact_notes: Equipment damage (web strap cut on upper floating beam); operational impact from unplanned equipment failure during maintenance activity; potential safety risk to personnel in lift operations area.

evidence.quote: Lack of safety procedures to document the presence of an O2 meter and constrain movement of the OMS pod resulted in a close call.
During rotation of the L/H OMS pod (LP04) an O2 meter was heard rolling inside the pod.
Add a safety call-out to the wad authorizing installation of an O2 meter in flight hardware, constraining movement of hardware with meter installed.

impact_notes: Close call during pod rotation; potential for meter damage or pod structural damage if movement had continued unchecked; operational delay to safely remove meter from vertical position.

evidence.quote: Failure to require tethering of a flow meter used to perform leak checks resulted in damage to the orbiter when the meter fell
Add handles and tethering aids to the flow meters prior to use in or around the orbiter
The tygon hose became tangled and the leak detector fell and hit the side of the vehicle damaging seven tiles

impact_notes: Damage to seven orbiter thermal protection system tiles requiring repair; equipment damage; operational disruption for leak check procedures.

evidence.quote: APU hydrazine isolation valve assembly was removed and sent to the shipping section of logistics without being properly packaged for safe handling. There was no Q.A. check sheet and no decontamination tags
Revise/update standard practice instructions (SPI) to include bagging and tagging of components exposed to hypergols prior to transporting them
conditional decontamination tag with the part indicating it was from a fuel system, conditionally decontaminated but absorbed or trapped fuel could out-gas

impact_notes: Close call with hydrazine exposure hazard; valve tested positive for residual hydrazine requiring immediate safing and OPF emergency response.

evidence.quote: Batteries are of a new design still under qualification testing.
Use of batteries that have not completed qualification testing caused employees to receive caustic electrolyte exposure
Institute requirements that will prevent the use of batteries that have not completed qualification testing.

impact_notes: Two employees received caustic electrolyte chemical exposure requiring medical evaluation; safety incident during critical flight hardware installation for Space Shuttle mission.

evidence.quote: Lack of a safety cut-out switch on the proof load test fixture resulted in a "close call" when a "D" ring was distorted (over pulled).
The tensile puller pump motor stopped and the operator shut down the machine immediately and discovered tensile puller over pulled the "D" ring, deforming it.

impact_notes: Test fixture hardware damage (distorted D-ring beyond salvage, 3 pins distorted); near-miss with potential for equipment failure or safety hazard during proof load testing operations.

evidence.quote: Failure to use valves capable of withstanding system pressures resulted in a 'close call' when a valve exploded.
The wheel valve o-ring does not always seat properly...seepage pressure (1500 psi) was greater than the design capacity of the vacuum pump isolation valve
Failure to establish a preventative maintenance program for two 6000 psi shutoff valves resulted in a close call when they began leaking

impact_notes: Valve burst at Pad B high pressure gas storage battery; close call with potential for catastrophic failure and facility damage or personnel injury.

evidence.quote: Failure to secure/tether a draeger pump during toxic vapor checks resulted in a 'close call' when the pump fell, striking the orbiter.
To ensure continued tetherability, provide a permanently attached tether for the draeger pump.

impact_notes: Close call incident with potential for orbiter damage; equipment fell from 207' level striking FRCS blanket area.

evidence.quote: Work authorizing document did not contain instructions or work stops to verify that there was no pressure on the flex hose.
Work authorizing document failed to include instructions/work stops to verify that there was no pressure on the system
System engineers should update work authorizing documents to include verification of zero pressure on systems prior to making repairs.

impact_notes: Gaseous nitrogen release containing N2H4 vapors during maintenance work; close call with potential personnel safety hazard and system damage.

evidence.quote: Lack of a preventative maintenance program for the OMS engine nozzle covers resulted in a "close call" when the covers' holding clamps failed.
The cover was found to have three holding clamps that were probably defective on installation at the OPF.
Safety gave the ok to continue the lift without the cover.

impact_notes: Cover fell 15 feet and suffered damage preventing reinstallation; safety risk during orbiter transfer operations; potential for injury or vehicle damage.

evidence.quote: Heat from hot weather caused the tape used to secure a tubehead assembly to soften allowing the tubehead to fall
Update documentation to require technicians to use rope, web straps or something of that nature in addition to tape

impact_notes: Close call with potential equipment damage; threaded strut sustained visible damage to 2-3 threads but no critical failure occurred.

evidence.quote: Due to mechanical design the interface connection is very poor. The LO2 interface is identical and has experienced leakage in the past.
Lack of adequate LH2 Leak detection at the 8 inch T-0 flexhose QD/interface could result in fire.
Undetected leakage could cause a major fire resulting loss of life or vehicle.

impact_notes: Potential loss of life and vehicle due to major fire from undetected cryogenic leakage at critical ground support interface.

evidence.quote: Failure of the series valve or clogging of the downstream filter could cause loss of bubbling. Loss of helium bubbling prior to 2% full or after 98% full...could result in damage due to geysering.
Develop a preventative maintenance program for the critical valves and filters in this system.

impact_notes: Loss of helium injection system could result in external tank damage due to geysering during propellant loading, risking mission safety and vehicle integrity.

evidence.quote: The A70-1289 work stands were designed for light, mobile work. During maintenance operations on space shuttle main engines (SSME) in the vertical position the stands must be split apart to clear engine ground support equipment (GSE). This r
Pic boards are utilized to span the open areas when the work stands are in the split configuration.

impact_notes: Reduced work stand stability and work area availability during critical SSME maintenance operations; operational workaround (pic boards) indicates design inadequacy for actual operational constraints.

evidence.quote: Access to the read-out ports in the GH2 fill line on side 1 of the MLPS is precarious. There are not adequate tie-off points for body harness and lanyards.

If a technician uses a ladder for access, or climbs on existing structures to make the reading, he/she is within inches of exterior handrails and above them. This places him/her about 40 feet above the vab floor or the pad surface.
Inadequate access to the vacuum read-out stations on the MLP LH2 system's vacuum jacketed lines could lead to personnel injury.

impact_notes: Personnel safety hazard: technicians required to access read-out ports at 40 feet elevation with inadequate fall protection and tie-off points, creating risk of serious injury or fatality.

evidence.quote: Quick disconnects utilized for providing gasses to the space shuttle main engine (SSME) are identical to each other and can be cross-connected.
Should GN2 645 +/- 65 psig be introduced to the GH2 prepressurization system, flight hardware damage could occur.
This condition may or may not be visible on the firing room pressure gages depending upon the status of orbiter power.

impact_notes: Potential flight hardware damage to LH2 prepressurization system if wrong gas introduced; condition may be undetectable in real-time depending on orbiter power status, creating latent failure risk.

evidence.quote: The LH2 vent system valves are elevated 8 and 10 feet above ground with no safe access provided.

Operational personnel are frequently required to climb up on the 12", 14", and 18" vent lines to operate the valves. The use of safety belts/harnesses is not practical in this situation since the tether-off point is not available.

impact_notes: Personnel safety hazard: fall risk from 8-10 feet with no fall protection available during routine valve operations.

evidence.quote: Lack of a preventative maintenance program for the vertical installer resulted in failure of the installer to maintain control over the SSME.
This condition occurs when the piston sticks in the cylinder (due to lack of lubrication and/or foreign material in the cylinder).
Develop and implement a preventative maintenance program for the SSME vertical installer.

impact_notes: Failure to maintain positive control of SSME during installation/removal could result in uncontrolled engine movement, creating serious personnel safety hazard and potential damage to flight hardware.

evidence.quote: Inadequate grounding of the DCU memory loader (i.e., Ground cables not connected, connected improperly, or in deteriorated condition) could result in electric shock injury to personnel.
Update OMI to include a ground verification test of the DCU memory loader prior to beginning operations.

impact_notes: Personnel safety hazard: electrical shock injury risk from inadequate grounding of DCU memory loader equipment.

evidence.quote: The "C" frame support hook and support jack dowel require manual manipulation and experience interference during PBD opening/closing operations.
PBD damage may occur due to excessive forces incurred on it by jacking up PBD until its weight forces "C" hook to engage with support jack dowel.
The aft half of the L/H PBD was raised eight to ten inches higher than the position of the PBD forward segment.

impact_notes: Payload bay door structural damage requiring redesign of support mechanism; operational risk during critical ground operations.

evidence.quote: insufficient clearance was due to incorrect installation of support brackets caused by inaccurate OMI procedures
Scraping noises were heard and interference was observed. The operation was stopped while a deviation to the OMI was written
foam damage was unavoidable due to insufficient clearance

impact_notes: TPS foam damage to External Tank during demating operations; required deviation and operational workaround; potential impact to vehicle readiness and schedule.

evidence.quote: the line was approx. 0.5 inches shorter than required for optimal operation
Vibration during helicopter rotor operation may cause shortened hydraulic lines to become rub-fatigued/chafed at the spacer block contact points
Periodically inspect flight control lines and spacer blocks for chafing and replace as necessary

impact_notes: Loss of No.1 hydraulic system supporting helicopter flight controls constitutes an emergency condition with potential for catastrophic flight safety impact.

evidence.quote: Hydraulic pumping units leaked hydraulic fluid onto the insulation for several years.
Hot particles released by air-arcing blew into compartment 16B as the welder penetrated the MLP bottom panel, and the fluid ignited.
Design and install drain pans to catch and contain leaking hydraulic fluid beneath hydraulic components.

impact_notes: Fire ignition during critical launch platform operations; potential for personnel injury and mission delay.

evidence.quote: It is unknown when or who removed the bolts.
Documentation lacking cross references between serial numbers and model numbers may result in inadequate configuration or missing hardware.
Improper work authorization contributed to the unauthorized removal of six attachment bolts

impact_notes: Six critical attachment bolts missing from SRM aft segment lifting bracket discovered during assembly operations; potential for catastrophic structural failure during handling and transport if not detected.

evidence.quote: Shocks/vibrations induced into a magnetic liquid level gauge may affect the position of individual flag indicators and lead to erroneous readings.
Fluid contamination may cause a restriction of the float assembly movement within the chamber, leading to an inaccurate reading.
Fluid in the LT having a SG which is less than the float assembly is weighted for, indicates a fluid level lower than actual, and may lead to a tank overfill.

impact_notes: Tank overfill event with hydrazine (toxic propellant) resulted in liquid spray from vent hose; potential for personnel exposure and environmental contamination.

evidence.quote: The move director called for a crane movement of "up at 1 in/sec" and the crane controller relayed the command to the crane operator. The actual lift rate was on the order of "up at 3 in/sec"

a momentary vertical load of 800 lbs was applied to the tank assembly. This load was immediately reduced and the tank bolts removed. The normal transfer load is made up of a 150 lb tank lifting fixture and a 235 lb tank weight.
Update OMI procedure to utilize hydraset units (in place of cranes) during transferring of loads to avoid overloading tank assemblies.

impact_notes: Momentary 800 lb overload (3.4× nominal 235 lb tank weight) applied to tank assembly during critical removal operation; potential for structural damage or failure if load had been sustained.

evidence.quote: The speed associated with crane commands is a matter of operator judgement and may result in an overload to the instrument pointing system (IPS).
If the joystick is in the null position when the button is depressed, load movement will occur with speed dependent on stick position when the brake is released.
An overload at 900 lbs in the IPS resulted when 'slow micro' was requested and the brake button was depressed with the joystick not in the null position.

impact_notes: Overload condition (900 lbs vs. intended 337 lbs) to instrument pointing system; potential equipment damage and mission impact.

evidence.quote: Visual observation must be maintained at all times during hoisting operations due to extremely limited clearances between the orbiter and facilities.
Excessive loads may be imposed on the stabilizing frame assembly while hoisting during mate/demate operations.
Maintain crane lift cables vertical and sling assembly level to prevent excessive loads imposed on stabilizing frame center segment.

impact_notes: Risk of structural failure to stabilizing frame during critical orbiter mate/demate operations; potential for orbiter damage or safety hazard to personnel and facilities.

evidence.quote: With no overtemp switches installed, excessive heat from the payload bay duct heaters burned through the duct insulation, causing severe damage with a potential for orbiter damage.

The payload bay duct heater adds measured quantities of heat independently to each duct system...Excessive heat from payload bay duct heaters may cause severe damage to ducting, insulation, and orbiter payload bay.
Design and install high temperature cutoff switches in payload bay duct heaters for overtemp emergency shutdown.

impact_notes: Potential orbiter damage from thermal runaway in payload bay duct system; actual damage to ducting insulation occurred.

evidence.quote: Vent door observers are always on station when a vent door is repositioned for a test or TPS tile/thermal barrier work.
Normal purge air applied to the orbiter compartment while the vent doors are closed may cause major orbiter damage.
Specify in work document that vent doors must remain in standard purge configuration and all on-board vent door circuit breakers remain 'open'.

impact_notes: Overpressurization could cause major orbiter structural damage. Incident prevented by procedural controls and observer presence, but underlying risk required formalized work document specification.

evidence.quote: If the HIM 161 direct mode fails, there is a potential for partial/total loss of chiller GN2 control and subsequent orbiter/payload damage.
Design a remote back-up capability to allow chiller control from the redundant HIM 213 card in the event of HIM 161 failure.

impact_notes: Potential for partial or total loss of chiller GN2 control resulting in high temperature/humidity damage to orbiter and payload.

evidence.quote: the speed attained after leaving the slow travel transition is excessive. The operator must manipulate a manual valve to reduce air flow
High impact stops from the rapid movement of the "Y" curtain wall during retraction may cause personnel injury and/or damage to payloads and facility
Install and utilize a flow control valve in the air winch supply line to permit speed regulation

impact_notes: Personnel injury risk and potential damage to payloads and facility infrastructure from uncontrolled high-speed retraction impacts.

evidence.quote: Transfer of heat from brakes to the MLG tires after maximum braking could increase tire pressure and result in rupture/blowout.
provide crew training with braking techniques for various landing profiles. Prior to orbiter leaving the OPF, perform anti-skid brake inspections
Excessive tire wear due to maximum braking or FOD may reduce strength of tire structure and cause rise in tire pressure, resulting in rupture.

impact_notes: Explosive tire rupture could injure personnel and damage orbiter hydraulic system, ground support equipment, and cause hydraulic fire in hot wheel well area.

evidence.quote: If the vacuum servicing unit is left connected to the suction line, inadvertent opening of manual valve A81992 could subject the pump to 935 psig from the cryo valve panel. This is beyond the unit's capability
Failure to protect the vacuum servicing unit from inadvertent actuations of the 935 psig cryo valve panel could result in damage to flight hardware
Install a pressure actuated check valve to prevent the high cryo pressure from affecting the vacuum servicing unit

impact_notes: Potential damage to flight hardware and ground support equipment; pump oil expulsion through vent system could contaminate systems or create safety hazard.

evidence.quote: The check valve in the inlet line of this vacuum pump has been unable to prevent reverse flow of air through the pump and into the system.
replacement of components not compatible with the fuel cell coolant FC-40 and electrical system modifications have been made to meet shuttle program use requirements
if the system's volume is sufficiently large, oil vapor may be sucked from the pump into the system

impact_notes: Reverse flow of air and oil vapor into fuel cell coolant system could compromise coolant purity and fuel cell operation; Apollo-derived hardware inadequately adapted for Shuttle FC-40 coolant requirements.

evidence.quote: Similar adjacent connections on the ECS pressure distribution system allow for the possibility of misconnected lines resulting in damage.
Pressure lines could be misconnected and result in damage to flight hardware or injury to personnel.
Install keyed connectors to prevent misconnection of pressure lines.

impact_notes: Potential for flight hardware damage and personnel injury from misconnected pressure lines during pre-operations setup.

evidence.quote: Failure to establish a preventative maintenance program for the PRSD pod latching mechanism has resulted in damage to the thermal protection system.
This mishap was caused by a worn or deformed stop pin in the pod latching mechanism. Failure has occurred on several occasions.
A scupper to support the pod has not been provided for Dryden Flight Research Center.

impact_notes: Damage to thermal protection system from repeated latching mechanism failures due to worn stop pins; multiple failure occurrences indicate systemic operational risk.

evidence.quote: flexhoses are too long for such a short connection area, hose kinking occurs
can cause damage to the orbiter avionics systems due to overheating because of loss of freon cooling

impact_notes: Loss of freon cooling to orbiter avionics due to hose kinking could cause system overheating and mission-critical avionics failure.

evidence.quote: Fixed hydrogen leak detection sensors monitor the fuel cell servicing/deservicing system areas for H2 leaks which could result in damage to vehicle/facility or personnel injury.
A PRSD GH2 leak may result in accumulation of flammable vapors in orbiter compartments or near umbilicals, causing injury or damage.
Provide periodic inspection/preventative maintenance on fixed hydrogen leak detection sensors and thoroughly inspect for flammable/toxic vapors.

impact_notes: Potential for personnel injury or vehicle/facility damage from flammable hydrogen vapor accumulation in orbiter compartments and near umbilicals during fuel cell servicing operations.

evidence.quote: Failure of 250/175-ton crane DC power loop or DC control loop critical components could result in damage/loss of flight hardware/GSE or injury.
Perform periodic preventative maintenance, testing, and pre-operational checks on 250/175-ton cranes to ensure support lifting operation readiness.

impact_notes: Potential loss of flight hardware (SRB, ET, orbiter) or ground support equipment; risk of personnel injury from crane failure at heights up to 466 feet.

evidence.quote: Prior to fuel transfer operations, inspect grounding reel for deterioration resulting in poor common ground between fueling tanker and transporter.
An ignition source (spark, heat, or flame) present during a PCT diesel fueling operation could possibly ignite the diesel fuel.

Fueling operations are prohibited during electrical storms within five miles of the transfer site. Flame or spark producing devices, such as matches, lighters, or smoking, are also prohibited in this area.

impact_notes: Potential fire/explosion hazard during fueling operations at Kennedy Space Center payload processing facilities; could result in loss of payload, vehicle, facility damage, and personnel injury.

evidence.quote: The crane operator is not able to control the load or terminate all power in an emergency situation because the 4-ton crane lacks required safety devices.
The 4-ton monorail crane lacks a local emergency power disconnect device which increases potential for flight hardware damage or personnel injury.
The crane operator does not have direct access to the main circuit breaker in the event of crane speed control system failure or other hoisting failure.

impact_notes: Potential for flight hardware damage and personnel injury due to inability to emergency stop crane in failure scenarios.

evidence.quote: Excessive noise levels during GOX vent arm air receivers bleed manifold venting operations may damage personnel hearing.
noise level of 126.5 DBA. OSHA 1910.95, occupational noise exposure, limits noise exposure for fifteen minutes or less to 115 DBA.
Install muffler on outlet vent to operate both air receivers during bleed operations and reduce noise level to within OSHA regulations.

impact_notes: Personnel hearing loss risk during routine bleed-down operations; OSHA non-compliance (126.5 vs 115 DBA limit).

evidence.quote: During and after landing, there are no means aboard the space shuttle orbiter for detection of hazardous gases. A hazardous gas sensing capability internal to orbiter compartments does not exist until the T-0 hookup
An undetected accumulation of toxic/flammable vapors in orbiter compartments after landing may cause damage/loss of orbiter/personnel.
